messages.message:
Patch Set 1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

unfortunately we can't use this with ZFS. the issue is that "skipped" write will likely turn into EIO as the checksums won't match.
I'm afraid the solution needs to be per-fs and even worse I'm not sure we can "freeze" DMU pool from the userspace :(

Patch Set 1:

actually there is a hidden command to freeze pools: spool freeze <poolname>. please try with that.

Patch Set 3: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(16 inline comments)

I like the general implementation, and it is excellent that we don't need to patch the kernel.  Some details to be cleaned up.

Main issues that need to be fixed are interoperability for older versions of Lustre, and whether this works on older kernels or not.

Patch Set 3:

To clarify, my concern is that we can't change the test framework code to require the new dm-flakey mechanism since this will break interop testing if this mechanism is not working on older versions of Lustre (which are configured by the test-framework code on the server and will not have the right DM device names, nor the check_and_add_to_md() function in its test-framework.sh file).

This also depends on whether the dm-flakey mechanism works on all supported kernels (RHEL6, SLES11 SP2, etc).  I see that someone from Red Hat committed the "drop_writes" mode to dm_flakey in the upstream kernel version v3.0-6027-gb26f5e3, and checking my local kernel I see that the "drop_writes" feature appears in 2.6.32-279.5.1.el6 (RHEL 6.3 and presumably later), but not in linux-2.6.32-131.6.1 (RHEL 6.1) so we should probably be OK on this front.

Patch Set 6: Code-Review-1

I find this approach unreliable. could we try to use snapshots instead? say, when rdonly is activated, we take a snapshot and the filesystem is keep going. when rdonly is reset, we rollback to that snapshot. the principal difference is that when rdonly is active we still see correct filesystem while with the current approach we are running on a corrupted filesystem and every time we have to read a block with skipped write we risk to find the filesystem corrupted. we're lucky enough to use this for years, but sometimes it fails..

Hi Oleg,

If one of the following three conditions was met, then the dm device will be removed:

                if [[ -n ${!failover_host} && ${!failover_host} != ${!host} ]]||
                        $CLEANUP_DM_DEV || [[ $facet = fs* ]]; then
                        dm_cleanup_dev $facet
                fi

For the first condition, it means there is a failover node for the current node. For the second condition, CLEANUP_DM_DEV is set with false by default. And for the third condition, it means the facet name is starting with “fs”.

Hi Oleg,

The debug codes showed that:

 + local host=mds1_HOST
 + local failover_host=mds1failover_HOST
 + [[ -n centos6-16.localnet ]]
 + [[ centos6-16.localnet != centos6-16.localnet ]]
 + true
 + dm_cleanup_dev mds1

The "true" value was from variable CLEANUP_DM_DEV. However, that variable was only set to "true" in the following code before running sanity sub-tests:

 CLEANUP_DM_DEV=true formatall

For bash, variable assignments preceding commands affect only that command, even builtins and functions. However, for sh, all variable assignments preceding commands are global.

So, the debug codes showed that bash was not used somehow.

In my CentOS 7.2 test node, while running the following command, I hit the same issue as above:

 VERBOSE=true SLOW=yes REFORMAT=yes ONLY=17m sh sanity.sh

After changing "sh" to "bash" as follows, the test passed:

 VERBOSE=true SLOW=yes REFORMAT=yes ONLY=17m bash sanity.sh

Could you please try "bash"? Thank you.

Patch Set 18:

Hi Andreas and Oleg,

I found in Lustre test suites, there are many places where the codes use the way of variable assignments preceding commands/functions, such as:

 REFORMAT="yes" check_and_setup_lustre
 RPC_MODE=true init_test_env
 FAIL_ON_ERROR=false check_runas_id_ret $myUID $myGID $myRUNAS
 HSMTOOL_NOERROR=true copytool_setup $SINGLEAGT
 HSM_ARCHIVE_PURGE=false copytool_setup
 ……
So, if we use “sh” to run the scripts, the values of those variables will be set global, which is not expected.

commit.subject: LU-684 tests: replace dev_read_only patch with dm-flakey
commit.message:
LU-684 tests: replace dev_read_only patch with dm-flakey

The dev_read_only kernel patch is mainly used for testing,
in order to simulate a server crash for ldiskfs by discarding
all of the writes to the device.

Since Linux kernel 3.0, this testing functionality can be
simulated by using "dm-flakey" target for device-mapper,
which supports a "drop_writes" parameter that could be used
in place of our dev_read_only kernel patch.
